title: Is Everyone Hanging Out Without Me? (And Other Concerns)

chapters.title: Connections open doors
chapters.text:
In high school, Mindy Kaling found genuine joy in Latin class, movie nights with close girlfriends, drawn-out crushes on older boys who barely knew she existed, and quiet evenings with her family. She was nobody's idea of a rising star back then — often invisible in the school social scene — so her later success had nothing to do with teenage popularity or a charmed upbringing.

She never went to a party where alcohol flowed, and no one ever offered her weed. At sixteen she learned that marijuana and pot were the same thing — not from peers, but from a rerun of the 2012 comedy 21 Jump Street. Weeknights belonged to homework; her parents allowed almost no weekday socializing. Friday reward: The X-Files, if she stayed out of trouble. Seinfeld on Thursday was reserved for weeks when she'd aced her tests. Those limits frustrated her because the glossy, effortless high school life on TV bore no resemblance to hers.

Discipline and sustained effort can carry you far, even when it means postponing some of the fun youth promises.

This summary walks through Kaling's path — romance, female friendship, bullying as a teenager, and her climb into Hollywood. Ready for a push toward your own ambitions? Keep reading.

chapters.title: Kids can wound you deeply
chapters.text:
Kaling has been round-bodied for as long as she can remember — except at birth, when she weighed six pounds. By first grade she hit sixty-eight pounds. She first tried slimming down at fourteen, then again at nineteen. The fourteen-year-old effort traced back to Daunte Diallo, a Senegalese basketball star adored by teachers and classmates alike. Diallo verbally tormented her about her weight, insisting she could be gorgeous if she dropped pounds, then escalated to calling her a whale until dieting consumed her thoughts.

Bullies follow no honor code. ~ Mindy Kaling

Pre-internet, she had few weight-loss resources, so she improvised: half portions, no dessert, zero exercise. Thirty pounds vanished in two months. She wanted the teasing to stop and to reclaim access to the freshmen hangout space. Diallo kept mocking her anyway.

Treat people gently — you rarely see the wars they're fighting.

One hallway encounter still stings: Diallo and friends blocked her path while he puffed his cheeks and said, "Remember when Mindy was like a whale?" His crew laughed; she didn't. He left school junior year, which was a relief.

The nineteen-year-old weight loss mattered more because she finally added movement. At Dartmouth in 1999, her best friend Brenda Withers taught her to run — twenty minutes of walking punctuated by sprints between lamp posts or street signs. Another thirty pounds gone in a semester. After that, being called chubby no longer ruined her sleep.

chapters.title: Early steps toward adulthood
chapters.text:
Kaling thinks teenage girls should stop obsessing over peak popularity or being the school's top actress or athlete. After graduation, almost nobody cares about the dramas that once felt enormous. Driven teens should know that staying low-key is fine. Besides delighting your parents, you still have years to discover what you actually want.

Big leaps forward demand attention on where you're headed, not where you've been.

In July 2001 she relocated to New York with Brenda and Jocelyn. All three were jobless, crammed into a railroad apartment in Windsor Terrace, Brooklyn. Audition after audition ended in rejection.

By October, at twenty-two, she landed babysitting work for two sweet girls, Dylan and Haley. She excelled because she stayed open to whatever the kids wanted to talk about. Babysitting, for her, meant nurturing children and raiding their snacks.

chapters.title: From Brenda and Mindy to Matt and Ben
chapters.text:
Babysitting finally covered the bills, but Kaling and Brenda — then substituting in public schools — craved creative output. No one was hiring them as actresses or screenwriters, so they decided to manufacture their own opportunities instead of waiting for permission.

They blocked one hour daily for writing, though early on they mostly sprawled on the couch watching reality TV. Gradually they built sketch characters, rehearsing on commutes and while getting dressed. Their standout bit had Withers imitating Matt Damon while Kaling played Ben Affleck — the early-2000s stars of Good Will Hunting. That impersonation grew into a play they submitted to the New York International Fringe Festival.

When no door opens, build one yourself.

Their debut Matt & Ben production had no director and no supporting cast — they couldn't pay for either. In 2002 the Fringe named it best among five hundred entries. The New Yorker called it goofy, funny, and oddly believable, praising one of the finest male-bonding stories since Damon and Pythias — or Oscar and Felix.

Life shifted fast. Producers wanted an off-Broadway transfer. They secured a director and a real budget. The runtime allowed two shows nightly; famous actors and actresses attended, boosting sales enough to add a third performance each evening.

chapters.title: Dating boys versus men
chapters.text:
Kaling's first real date came at twenty-five with Peter Parker, a comedy writer. One evening stretched into four. His quaint Spanish ranch house in Hollywood was the first boyfriend residence that felt like an actual home. The romance ended, but she spotted a trait she'd later rank essential: zero terror of commitment. For Kaling, commitment spans more than romance — it's pledging to keep figuring life out.

Until thirty she dated boys, not men, because men intimidated her. Men know their desires, make firm plans, and own alarm clocks. They sleep on real mattresses, tip well, and visit dentists. They buy fresh shampoo instead of watering down the bottle. They kiss without a ten-minute preamble about kissing intentions.

Boys charm. They carry backpacks to the office and trail off mid-sentence in an appealing way. Roommates cut their hair; they can stuff a life into a gym bag and bolt to Brooklyn for a gig. Cash-poor most weeks, they splurge on Colorado music festivals when flush. They struggle to shift tone between friends and parents, treating Mom and Dad like peers while letting them pick up dinner tabs. Odd-hour diner chats at 3 a.m. suit their irregular schedules — charming until you're past thirty.

Emotional maturity anchors stable partnerships.

chapters.title: Power found in unlikely corners
chapters.text:
Amid the 2007 writers' strike, Kaling purchased a Spanish-style home in Los Angeles. A dedicated office exists, yet her real workspace is bed — laptop perched on fleece or a Notre Dame sweatshirt, resembling, in her words, someone on medical leave. Silence at home keeps her attention from splintering.

I'm kind of a mess. I did, however, fulfill a childhood dream of writing and acting in television and movies. ~ Mindy Kaling

The writers' room fed her gregarious side; she's built for conversation. At the studio, her assigned office became a junk closet. Online rabbit holes wreck her schedule — researching almond milk can devolve into Donner Party history and group chats. She estimates one productive hour for every seven spent "preparing": pacing hallways, stomping cardboard flat, rewatching BBC Pride & Prejudice bonus features, assembling snack spreads, and replaying toddler choreography to Beyoncé.

Brilliance doesn't immunize you against distraction — forgive yourself when the day vanishes into errands disguised as craft.

chapters.title: Relationships that built a career
chapters.text:
Kaling credits screenwriter Greg Daniels and his wife for spotting her talent at a Matt & Ben performance. Within a week she joined The Office (2005–2013) as a staff writer.

The job terrified her initially. She was funny but green among veteran comedy writers, constantly afraid Daniels would notice the gap and fire her. Over time, as with any coworkers you see daily, friendships formed on set. Season one's core writers included Daniels, Paul Lieberstein, Mike Schur, B. J. Novak, and Kaling herself, with Larry Wilmore and Lester Lewis consulting three days weekly. They filmed in a remote industrial corner of Panorama City in the San Fernando Valley. Critical praise surged in season two thanks to standout episodes.

Breakthroughs usually blend personal ability with mentors who believe in you.

Later she wrote for Saturday Night Live during the gap between The Office's second and third seasons — Mike Schur recommended her. The SNL room held Amy Poehler, Seth Meyers, Rachel Dratch, and Tina Fey, often pairing off. Knowing nobody, Kaling hesitated to pitch ideas to colleagues.

chapters.title: Escaping parties without fanfare
chapters.text:
Kaling never had a one-night stand and once barely understood what "hooking up" meant. She adores the "Irish exit" — slipping away without announcing goodbye.

Her method involves mild deception. She'll loudly ask, "Where's the bathroom?" with the body language of a lost tourist, then grab her coat and leave. Or she claims her car lights are on or the doors unlocked. Vehicles make perfect exit excuses — car trouble bores everyone, so nobody follows up. She isn't too busy or too cool for pleasantries; at gatherings over thirty people she simply skips the endless hello-goodbye circuit.

Setting boundaries for your own comfort is fine when you still treat others with respect.

chapters.title: What marriage looks like to Mindy
chapters.text:
Shakespeare found courtship more dramatic than marriage itself, so his plays often end at the altar. Growing up, Kaling saw roughly a quarter of classmates' parents divorce. As an adult she's met plenty of people trapped in unhappy marriages or fresh from splits. Her own parents thrive because they share obsessions — they can discuss the same topics indefinitely without friction.

Those passions include rose bushes, mulch, shrub placement, rhododendrons, Men of a Certain Age, Piers Morgan, vanilla milkshakes, then bed.

Marriage can be gorgeous, but it demands steady work.

Despite cultural cynicism, Kaling thinks marriage shouldn't feel impossibly complex. Couples should fight to preserve what they have because partnership is a privilege many crave yet can't find; plenty of singles envy what married pairs share.

She believes every contentedly married person should feel grateful — like the flower-crowned finale of a Shakespeare comedy.

Did you know? Mike Schur once gave Kaling a pre-written eulogy for her eventual funeral.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Today Kaling is forty-two with two kids — Katherine and Spencer. She produces, acts, and still calls herself chubby without apology. Pregnancy meant heavy yoga, walking, and jogging to drop baby weight; between projects she exercises four or five days weekly from her Los Angeles home. In 2020 she and Lang Fisher debuted Never Have I Ever, tracing a first-generation Indian-American teenager through the chaos of growing up — drawn partly from Kaling's own adolescence.

She voices Val Little on Disney's 2021 animated series Monster at Work, following mechanic Tylor Tuskmon's bid to reach Monsters Inc.'s Laugh Floor. Val serves on the devoted Facilities Team (MIFT). Upcoming work includes co-writing Legally Blonde 3 with Dan Goor and an untitled Lakers workplace comedy spotlighting the franchise's family owners and the staff behind the seventeen-time NBA champions.

Try this


• Protect your passion: Keep the long game in view even when side gigs pay the bills.
• Pressure-test ideas with trusted friends: Outside eyes sharpen blind spots.
• Surround yourself with kindred spirits: Shared enthusiasm sustains momentum when motivation dips.
